name: Solar System
description: Explore our cosmic neighborhood — from the scorching surface of Mercury to the icy depths of the Kuiper Belt. Master planetary properties, discover what makes each moon unique, and trace humanity’s greatest space missions.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the largest planet in the solar system?
facts.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet by far — its mass is more than twice that of all other planets combined. Source: Wikipedia/Jupiter
facts.statement: Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system.
facts.wowFactor: Jupiter is so massive that all other planets could fit inside it at the same time — with room to spare.
facts.visualDescription: Colossal striped gas giant dwarfing the tiny rocky planets beside it, swirling orange and brown bands across its surface under starlit space

variants.question: Jupiter holds which distinction among solar system planets?
Which planet has a mass more than twice all others combined?
True or false: Jupiter is larger than all other solar system planets combined.
The largest planet in the solar system is ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has a centuries-old storm called the Great Red Spot?
facts.explanation: Jupiter's Great Red Spot is an anticyclonic storm wider than Earth that has been observed for at least 350 years. Source: Wikipedia/Jupiter
facts.statement: Jupiter has a centuries-old storm called the Great Red Spot, wider than Earth.
facts.wowFactor: The Great Red Spot is a storm that has been raging for centuries — longer than the United States has existed.
facts.visualDescription: Giant rust-red oval storm vortex swirling on Jupiter's banded surface, surrounded by cream and brown cloud belts, tiny Earth shown beside it for scale

variants.question: The Great Red Spot is a massive storm on which planet?
Which planet hosts a storm system larger than Earth?
True or false: Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm larger than Earth.
___ has a centuries-old storm known as the Great Red Spot.

facts.quizQuestion: As of 2026, how many known moons does Jupiter have?
facts.explanation: As of March 2026, Jupiter has 101 confirmed moons — the second-highest of any planet. Saturn now holds the record with 285. Source: Wikipedia/Jupiter
facts.statement: Jupiter has 101 known moons as of 2026.
facts.wowFactor: Jupiter has so many moons it has its own miniature solar system — including some larger than the planet Mercury.
facts.visualDescription: Jupiter looming huge in deep space with dozens of small moons orbiting at various distances, four large Galilean moons clearly visible nearby

variants.question: Jupiter's 101 known moons make it which type of object?
True or false: Jupiter has more than 50 known moons.
True or false: Jupiter has the most confirmed moons of any planet.
Jupiter has ___ known moons as of 2026.

facts.quizQuestion: Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto are moons of which planet?
facts.explanation: These four are the Galilean moons of Jupiter, discovered by Galileo Galilei in 1610. Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system. Source: Wikipedia/Jupiter
facts.statement: Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto are the four Galilean moons of Jupiter.
facts.wowFactor: Galileo spotted these four moons in 1610 with a crude telescope — it was the first proof that not everything in the sky orbits Earth.
facts.visualDescription: Four distinct moons lined up beside massive striped Jupiter: volcanic orange Io, icy blue-white Europa, grey Ganymede, and cratered Callisto

variants.question: Which moon of Jupiter is the largest moon in the solar system?
Galileo Galilei discovered four large moons of which planet in 1610?
True or false: Europa is one of Jupiter's four Galilean moons.
Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto orbit ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet could fit all other solar system planets inside it?
facts.explanation: Jupiter's volume is so vast that every other planet in the solar system could fit inside it simultaneously. Its radius is 69,911 km — roughly 11 times Earth's. Source: Wikipedia/Jupiter
facts.statement: Jupiter is large enough to fit all other solar system planets inside it.
facts.wowFactor: You could stuff every other planet — Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune — all at once inside Jupiter and still have room left over.
facts.visualDescription: Enormous Jupiter with dotted outlines of all other planets packed inside it like toys in a jar, showing their relative tiny sizes

variants.question: Jupiter's volume is large enough to hold all other planets — how many times wider is it than Earth?
All other planets combined would fit inside which single planet?
True or false: All other planets in the solar system could fit inside Jupiter.
___ is large enough to contain all other solar system planets.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet was the first discovered to have rings besides Saturn?
facts.explanation: Jupiter was confirmed to have a faint ring system by Voyager 1 in 1979, surprising scientists. Unlike Saturn's prominent ice rings, Jupiter's rings are thin and made of tiny dust particles. Uranus and Neptune were later found to have rings too.
facts.statement: Jupiter was the first planet discovered to have rings besides Saturn.
facts.wowFactor: Jupiter's rings are so faint they were invisible from Earth until Voyager 1 flew past in 1979!
facts.visualDescription: Jupiter with thin ghostly rings barely visible around its equator, dimly lit against dark space, dwarfed by Saturn's brilliant rings shown faintly in the background

variants.question: Jupiter's faint rings were discovered by which space mission?
Which planet surprises most people by having faint, hard-to-see rings?
True or false: Saturn is the only planet in the solar system with rings.
___ has faint rings discovered by the Voyager 1 spacecraft in 1979.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is famous for its large, visible ring system?
facts.explanation: Saturn's rings are the most extensive and visible planetary ring system in the solar system, easily seen through a small telescope. They extend up to 282,000 km from Saturn. Source: Wikipedia/Saturn
facts.statement: Saturn is famous for its spectacular, clearly visible ring system.
facts.wowFactor: Saturn's rings stretch so wide that they could fit six Earths side by side across them — yet they are often less than 10 metres thick.
facts.visualDescription: Saturn's golden-brown globe encircled by brilliant flat rings of white and grey ice, casting a sharp shadow across the planet's surface in sunlight

variants.question: Saturn's ring system is made mostly of what material?
Which planet can be identified through a backyard telescope by its rings?
True or false: Saturn's rings are visible through a small telescope from Earth.
___ is the planet most famous for its spectacular ring system.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has a density low enough to float on water?
facts.explanation: Saturn's average density is 0.687 g/cm³ — lower than water's 1.0 g/cm³. It is the only planet in the solar system that would float if placed in a large enough ocean. Source: Wikipedia/Saturn
facts.statement: Saturn is the only planet that would float on water, with a density of 0.687 g/cm³.
facts.wowFactor: Saturn is made mostly of hydrogen and helium gas — it's so light that if you could find an ocean big enough, the entire planet would bob on the surface.
facts.visualDescription: Cartoon-style Saturn with its rings sitting on a vast ocean surface, floating like a beach ball, water lapping gently at its equator under a blue sky

variants.question: Saturn's density is 0.687 g/cm³ — lower than what everyday substance?
Which planet is the only one in our solar system less dense than water?
True or false: Saturn is the only planet in the solar system that could float on water.
___ has a density lower than water and would float if placed in a large enough ocean.

facts.quizQuestion: Which moon of Saturn has a thick atmosphere and methane lakes?
facts.explanation: Titan is Saturn's largest moon and the only moon in the solar system with a dense atmosphere. Its surface has lakes and rivers of liquid methane. Source: Wikipedia/Saturn
facts.statement: Saturn's moon Titan has a thick atmosphere and lakes of liquid methane on its surface.
facts.wowFactor: Titan has lakes and rain — but not of water. It rains liquid methane there, and rivers of it carve through orange haze-covered terrain.
facts.visualDescription: Orange haze-shrouded moon Titan with dark methane lakes gleaming on its surface, thick amber atmosphere glowing at its edge, Saturn's rings visible in the distant background

variants.question: Titan is the largest moon of which planet?
Which is the only moon in the solar system with a dense atmosphere?
True or false: Titan is the only moon in the solar system with a thick atmosphere.
Saturn's moon ___ has methane lakes and a thick orange atmosphere.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has rings made mostly of ice and rock particles?
facts.explanation: Saturn's rings are composed primarily of water ice particles ranging from tiny grains to chunks several metres across, mixed with rocky debris. Source: Wikipedia/Saturn
facts.statement: Saturn's rings are made mostly of water ice and rock particles.
facts.wowFactor: Saturn's rings are 99% pure water ice — more like a giant snow cloud than a solid disc. Many particles are as clean as fresh-fallen snow.
facts.visualDescription: Close-up cross-section of Saturn's rings showing glistening white ice chunks and pebbles of various sizes floating in formation against black space

variants.question: Saturn's rings are composed primarily of what two materials?
The rings of Saturn are made mainly of water ice — which mission studied them up close?
True or false: Saturn's rings are made mostly of water ice particles.
The rings of ___ are made mostly of water ice and rock debris.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet holds the record for most confirmed moons with 285?
facts.explanation: As of 2026, Saturn has 285 confirmed moons — the most of any planet. Notable moons include Titan, Enceladus, and Mimas. Source: Wikipedia/Saturn
facts.statement: Saturn has 285 confirmed moons — more than any other planet.
facts.wowFactor: Saturn overtook Jupiter for the moon record in 2023 when dozens of small new moons were confirmed — and astronomers are still finding more.
facts.visualDescription: Saturn surrounded by a swarm of moons at various orbital distances, from large Titan glowing orange to tiny dark specks of newly discovered moons, against a star-filled sky

variants.question: How many confirmed moons does Saturn have as of 2026?
Which planet recently surpassed Jupiter to hold the most confirmed moons?
True or false: Saturn has more confirmed moons than any other planet in 2026.
___ has 285 confirmed moons — the most of any planet.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
facts.explanation: Mercury orbits at just 0.387 AU from the Sun — less than half Earth's distance. Despite this extreme proximity, Mercury is NOT the hottest planet because it has no atmosphere to trap heat. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercury_(planet)
facts.statement: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun.
facts.wowFactor: Despite being the closest planet to the Sun, Mercury isn't even the hottest — Venus beats it because of its thick heat-trapping atmosphere.
facts.visualDescription: A small, battered grey world hugging a blazing sun, its surface pocked with craters, baking in harsh sunlight with no air to soften it.

variants.question: Mercury is the planet closest to what object?
Which planet has the shortest orbital period because it is nearest the Sun?
Mercury is the planet closest to the Sun. True or False?
_______ is the innermost planet in our solar system.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is the smallest in our solar system?
facts.explanation: Mercury has a radius of only 2,439.7 km — smaller than some moons, including Jupiter's Ganymede and Saturn's Titan. Pluto used to hold this title but was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercury_(planet)
facts.statement: Mercury is the smallest planet in the solar system.
facts.wowFactor: Mercury is so small that two of Jupiter's moons — Ganymede and Titan — are actually larger than it, yet they're just moons.
facts.visualDescription: A tiny cratered sphere, grey and lonely, dwarfed even by some of the giant moons orbiting other planets far away.

variants.question: What is the smallest planet in our solar system?
Which inner planet has a radius of only 2,440 km, smaller than some moons?
Mercury is the smallest planet in the solar system. True or False?
_______ is the smallest of the eight planets in the solar system.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has the highest surface temperature despite not being closest to the Sun?
facts.explanation: Venus averages 464°C — hotter than Mercury despite being farther from the Sun. Mercury has no atmosphere so heat escapes at night, causing swings from -180°C to 430°C. Venus's thick CO2 atmosphere traps heat via the greenhouse effect 24/7. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercury_(planet)
facts.statement: Venus is hotter than Mercury even though Mercury is closer to the Sun.
facts.wowFactor: The closest planet to the Sun is NOT the hottest. Venus wins because its thick CO2 blanket traps heat so effectively that it never cools down — not even at night.
facts.visualDescription: Two planets side by side: the closer one swinging between ice and fire with no air to hold heat, the farther one glowing a steady hellish orange under a permanent greenhouse sky.

variants.question: Mercury is NOT the hottest planet. Which planet beats it in surface temperature?
Which planet's thick CO2 atmosphere traps heat, making it hotter than Mercury?
Mercury, the closest planet to the Sun, is also the hottest planet. True or False?
_______ is the hottest planet, not Mercury, because of its greenhouse atmosphere.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is the most heavily cratered due to having no atmosphere?
facts.explanation: Mercury's surface is covered in ancient impact craters because it has virtually no atmosphere to burn up incoming meteoroids or slow them before impact, and no wind, rain, or geological activity to erode craters over time. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercury_(planet)
facts.statement: Mercury is the most cratered planet because it has no atmosphere to erode impacts.
facts.wowFactor: Every crater on Mercury is a permanent scar — without atmosphere, there's no rain, no wind, no erosion. A crater made a billion years ago looks almost the same today.
facts.visualDescription: A grey, ancient surface plastered with overlapping craters of every size, each one perfectly preserved like a fossil record of every cosmic collision Mercury ever survived.

variants.question: Why is Mercury the most cratered planet in the solar system?
distractors: It is closest to the Sun

variants.question: Which inner planet has preserved impact craters for billions of years due to lack of atmosphere?
Mercury has more visible craters than any other planet because it has no atmosphere. True or False?
_______ has no atmosphere to slow meteoroids, making it the most cratered planet.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has the highest average surface temperature at 464°C?
facts.explanation: Venus averages 464°C across its entire surface — hot enough to melt lead. Its thick CO2 atmosphere creates a runaway greenhouse effect, trapping solar heat far more effectively than any other planet. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Venus
facts.statement: Venus has the highest average surface temperature of any planet at 464°C.
facts.wowFactor: At 464°C, Venus is hot enough to melt lead. If you landed there, you wouldn't just burn — you'd be crushed by 90x Earth's atmospheric pressure at the same moment.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing orange sphere wrapped in thick yellow sulfuric acid clouds, its surface invisible from space but roasting at temperatures that would melt most metals.

variants.question: Venus is the hottest planet at approximately how many degrees Celsius?
Which planet's runaway greenhouse effect gives it a hotter surface than any other planet?
Venus has a higher average surface temperature than any other planet in the solar system. True or False?
_______ is the hottest planet, with an average surface temperature of 464°C.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet rotates backward compared to most other planets?
facts.explanation: Venus has a retrograde (backward) rotation — it spins clockwise when viewed from above the solar system's north pole, opposite to most planets. Its rotation period is -243 Earth days (the negative sign indicates retrograde). On Venus, the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Venus
facts.statement: Venus rotates backward (retrograde), so the Sun rises in the west there.
facts.wowFactor: On Venus, the Sun rises in the WEST. Its spin is so slow and so backward that if you lived there, a single day would last longer than its entire year.
facts.visualDescription: A planet spinning slowly in reverse, arrows showing its clockwise rotation while all other planets spin the other way — the stubborn rebel of the solar system.

variants.question: On Venus the Sun rises in the west because Venus has what type of rotation?
Which planet spins clockwise (retrograde) unlike most planets in the solar system?
Venus rotates in the opposite direction to most planets, making the Sun rise in the west. True or False?
_______ has a retrograde rotation, meaning the Sun rises in the west on its surface.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has a day that is longer than its year?
facts.explanation: Venus takes 243 Earth days to complete one rotation (one Venusian day) but only 225 Earth days to complete one orbit around the Sun (one Venusian year). So on Venus, a day is actually longer than a year — you'd celebrate a birthday before noon. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Venus
facts.statement: On Venus, a day (243 Earth days) is longer than a year (225 Earth days).
facts.wowFactor: On Venus, you'd celebrate your birthday before you finish a single day. A Venusian day lasts 243 Earth days but its year is only 225 Earth days long.
facts.visualDescription: A clock and a calendar side by side — the clock showing 243 days ticking by, the calendar already flipping to a new year at 225 days, showing time running backward on this strange world.

variants.question: How long is a day on Venus in Earth days?
Which planet's slow retrograde spin makes its rotation period longer than its orbital period?
A day on Venus lasts longer than a year on Venus. True or False?
On _______, a single day (243 Earth days) lasts longer than its entire year (225 Earth days).

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is called Earth's evil twin due to its thick CO2 atmosphere?
facts.explanation: Venus is similar to Earth in size and mass — about 95% of Earth's radius and 82% of its mass — but its thick CO2 atmosphere creates a runaway greenhouse effect, making it hellishly hostile. Scientists call it Earth's evil twin to highlight how similar planets can have radically different outcomes. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Venus
facts.statement: Venus is called Earth's evil twin because it is similar in size but has a deadly CO2 atmosphere.
facts.wowFactor: Venus and Earth are nearly the same size and formed from similar materials, yet one became a paradise for life and the other became a 464°C sulfuric acid hellscape. Same ingredients, very different outcomes.
facts.visualDescription: Two planets side by side — one blue and green with swirling white clouds, the other a sickly orange with crushing yellow acid clouds, both eerily similar in size but worlds apart in livability.

variants.question: Which planet is most similar to Earth in size but has a lethal CO2 atmosphere?
Earth's evil twin — similar in mass but with a runaway greenhouse effect — is which planet?
Venus is called Earth's evil twin because it is similar in size but has a toxic CO2 atmosphere. True or False?
_______ is nicknamed Earth's evil twin due to similar size but a crushing CO2 atmosphere.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is visible as the brightest natural object in Earth's sky after the Moon?
facts.explanation: Venus is the brightest planet in Earth's sky — and the third brightest object overall after the Sun and Moon. Its thick cloud cover reflects about 70% of sunlight. It appears near the horizon just before sunrise or after sunset, earning the names Morning Star and Evening Star. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Venus
facts.statement: Venus is the brightest planet in Earth's sky and is known as the Morning Star or Evening Star.
facts.wowFactor: Ancient cultures thought the Morning Star and Evening Star were two different objects. They didn't realize the dazzling light before dawn and the one after sunset were the same planet: Venus.
facts.visualDescription: A brilliant white point of light gleaming near the horizon in a pale blue twilight sky, far outshining every other star and planet around it.

variants.question: What nickname is given to Venus when it appears near the horizon before sunrise?
Which planet reflects about 70% of sunlight, making it the brightest planet visible from Earth?
Venus is the brightest planet in Earth's night sky and is sometimes called the Morning Star. True or False?
_______ is so bright in Earth's sky it earned the names Morning Star and Evening Star.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is the only one with confirmed liquid water on its surface?
facts.explanation: Earth is the only planet confirmed to have stable liquid water on its surface, covering about 71% of the planet. Mars has evidence of ancient water and ice, and Jupiter's moon Europa may have a subsurface ocean, but no other planet has liquid water at the surface. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth
facts.statement: Earth is the only planet with confirmed liquid surface water.
facts.wowFactor: Earth's surface is mostly water — and that's incredibly rare. Scan every planet we know of and not one other planet has a drop of liquid water just sitting on its surface right now.
facts.visualDescription: A blue marble floating in the black of space, its surface dominated by shimmering blue oceans, white cloud swirls, and small patches of green and brown land.

variants.question: Earth is the only planet with liquid water on its surface. What fraction of Earth is covered by water?
Which planet's surface temperature and atmospheric pressure allow liquid water to exist stably?
Earth is the only planet in the solar system confirmed to have liquid water on its surface. True or False?
_______ is the only planet with confirmed liquid surface water, covering 71% of its surface.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is third from the Sun, between Venus and Mars?
facts.explanation: Earth orbits at 1.0 AU from the Sun — by definition, since the AU (Astronomical Unit) is based on Earth's distance. It sits between Venus (0.723 AU) and Mars (1.524 AU), in the third orbital position from the Sun. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth
facts.statement: Earth is the third planet from the Sun, between Venus and Mars.
facts.wowFactor: The entire unit we use to measure distances in space — the Astronomical Unit — is literally defined as the distance from Earth to the Sun. Earth is the measuring stick of the solar system.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram of the inner solar system showing four planets in a line from the Sun: tiny Mercury, cloud-wrapped Venus, blue-green Earth, then rusty red Mars, each on their own orbital ring.

variants.question: Earth is the third planet from the Sun. Which planet is immediately inside Earth's orbit?
Earth orbits at exactly 1 AU from the Sun. Which planet is immediately outside Earth's orbit?
Earth is the third planet from the Sun, located between Venus and Mars. True or False?
_______ is the third planet from the Sun, sandwiched between Venus and Mars.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is the only one NOT named after a Greek or Roman god?
facts.explanation: Every other planet is named after a Roman deity: Mercury (messenger god), Venus (goddess of love), Mars (god of war), Jupiter (king of gods), Saturn (god of agriculture), Neptune (god of sea), Uranus (god of sky). Earth comes from Old English 'eorþe' and Germanic 'Erde' meaning ground or soil. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth
facts.statement: Earth is the only planet not named after a Greek or Roman god.
facts.wowFactor: Every planet was named after a Roman god — the messenger, the god of war, king of the gods — except the one humans actually live on. We just called it 'the ground.'
facts.visualDescription: A lineup of the planets, each with a tiny Roman deity symbol above it — wings for Mercury, a heart for Venus, a spear for Mars — all except the blue-green Earth, which just has the ground beneath it.

variants.question: Earth's name comes from Old English and Germanic words meaning what?
All other planets are named after Roman gods. Which planet breaks that tradition?
Earth is the only planet in the solar system not named after a Greek or Roman god. True or False?
_______ is the only planet whose name is not derived from Roman or Greek mythology.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has Olympus Mons, the tallest volcano in the solar system?
facts.explanation: Olympus Mons on Mars stands 21.9 km above the surrounding plains — nearly three times the height of Mount Everest (8.85 km). It is a shield volcano roughly the size of France. Its height is possible because Mars has no tectonic plates to shift the crust away from the hot spot over millions of years. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars
facts.statement: Mars has Olympus Mons, the tallest volcano and mountain in the entire solar system at 21.9 km.
facts.wowFactor: Olympus Mons is so enormous that if you stood on its summit, you wouldn't even know you were on a volcano — the slopes are so gentle and so vast that the edges would be below the horizon.
facts.visualDescription: An enormous gently sloping mountain rising from a rusty red Martian plain, dwarfing everything around it, its flat volcanic summit partially obscured by thin Martian clouds.

variants.question: Olympus Mons is the tallest mountain in the solar system. How tall is it in kilometers?
On which planet would you find Olympus Mons, a volcano nearly three times taller than Mount Everest?
Olympus Mons on Mars is the tallest known volcano in the solar system. True or False?
_______ is home to Olympus Mons, the tallest volcano in the solar system at 21.9 km tall.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is home to Valles Marineris, the largest canyon in the solar system?
facts.explanation: Valles Marineris stretches about 4,000 km across Mars — roughly the width of the United States — and reaches depths of up to 7 km. By comparison, Earth's Grand Canyon is only about 450 km long. It likely formed from tectonic rifting and erosion by ancient water. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars
facts.statement: Mars has Valles Marineris, the largest canyon in the solar system at 4,000 km long.
facts.wowFactor: Valles Marineris is so wide it would stretch coast to coast across the United States. Earth's Grand Canyon could fit inside one of its smaller side valleys.
facts.visualDescription: A vast rust-colored Martian surface split by an enormous gash — a canyon so long and wide it would be visible from orbit with the naked eye, its shadowed depths hinting at ancient geological violence.

variants.question: How long is Valles Marineris, the solar system's largest canyon on Mars, in kilometers?
Which planet has a canyon system over 4,000 km long — about as wide as the United States?
Valles Marineris on Mars is the largest canyon in the solar system. True or False?
_______ is home to Valles Marineris, the solar system's largest canyon at 4,000 km long.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has exactly two moons, named Phobos and Deimos?
facts.explanation: Mars has two small, irregularly shaped moons: Phobos and Deimos, named after the Greek gods of fear and dread. They are thought to be captured asteroids. Phobos orbits so close to Mars that it completes an orbit in just 7.7 hours — faster than Mars rotates — meaning it rises in the west and sets in the east. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars
facts.statement: Mars has two moons: Phobos and Deimos.
facts.wowFactor: Phobos orbits Mars so fast that it laps the planet — it rises in the west and sets in the east, and Martian observers would see it rise and set twice a day.
facts.visualDescription: Two small, potato-shaped rocky moons orbiting a rust-red planet — Phobos close and fast, Deimos small and distant, both pocked with craters, looking more like captured asteroids than proper moons.

variants.question: Phobos and Deimos are the two moons of which planet?
How many moons does Mars have?
Mars has two moons called Phobos and Deimos. True or False?
_______ has two moons: Phobos (Fear) and Deimos (Dread), named after Greek gods.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet shows dry riverbeds and other evidence of ancient liquid water?
facts.explanation: Mars shows extensive geological evidence of past liquid water: dried riverbeds, valley networks, ancient deltas, and minerals that only form in the presence of water. The Curiosity and Perseverance rovers have found lake-bed sediments. Mars likely had a warmer, wetter past billions of years ago before losing most of its atmosphere. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars
facts.statement: Mars shows geological evidence of ancient liquid water, including dry riverbeds and lake sediments.
facts.wowFactor: Billions of years ago, Mars may have had oceans. Today, NASA rovers drive through what were once lake beds, reading the story of an ancient wet world written in rock layers.
facts.visualDescription: A Martian landscape with ancient dry river channels carved into the rust-red surface, twisting like ghost rivers across dusty plains — the memory of a world that once had water.

variants.question: Mars shows evidence of ancient water in the form of dried riverbeds and lake sediments. Is this true?
The Curiosity rover found ancient lake-bed sediments on which planet, suggesting it once had liquid water?
Mars has geological evidence of ancient liquid water including dried riverbeds. True or False?
_______ has dry riverbeds and ancient lake sediments showing it once had liquid water.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet appears red because its surface is covered in iron oxide (rust)?
facts.explanation: Mars looks red because its surface and dust are rich in iron oxide — the same compound as rust on Earth. Over billions of years, iron in the Martian soil oxidized, coating the landscape in a reddish hue. This earned Mars its nickname 'the Red Planet.' Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars
facts.statement: Mars appears red because its surface is covered in iron oxide — essentially rust.
facts.wowFactor: Mars is literally rusty. The entire planet has been rusting for billions of years, and that red dust is so fine it gets kicked into the atmosphere during planet-wide dust storms that can last for months.
facts.visualDescription: A vivid rusty-red sphere rotating slowly in space, its surface a canvas of burnt orange and deep red with white polar ice caps at the top and bottom, iron oxide dust swirling in thin winds.

variants.question: Mars is red because of iron oxide on its surface. What is iron oxide commonly called?
Which planet earns the nickname the Red Planet due to iron oxide coating its surface?
Mars appears red because its surface is covered in iron oxide, the same compound as rust. True or False?
_______ is called the Red Planet because iron oxide (rust) covers its surface.

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the only spacecraft to have visited both Uranus and Neptune?
facts.explanation: Voyager 2 is the only spacecraft ever to fly past both Uranus (1986) and Neptune (1989), making it the sole human-made explorer of the ice giants. No mission has returned since.
facts.statement: Voyager 2 is the only spacecraft to have visited both Uranus and Neptune.
facts.wowFactor: We have literally only seen Uranus and Neptune up close once — with a single probe launched in 1977. No follow-up mission has ever been sent.
facts.visualDescription: A small silver probe drifting past two blue-green gas giants in the deep black of space, exhaust fading into starlight.

variants.quizQuestion: Voyager 2 is the only probe to fly past which two planets?
Which spacecraft completed the full Grand Tour of all four outer planets?
Which mission is the only one to have explored the ice giants Uranus and Neptune?
As of 2026, how many spacecraft have visited Neptune?

facts.quizQuestion: Which mission visited all four outer planets: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune?
facts.explanation: Voyager 2 completed the 'Grand Tour' by visiting Jupiter (1979), Saturn (1981), Uranus (1986), and Neptune (1989). A rare planetary alignment made this multi-planet trajectory possible — it won't recur for over 175 years.
facts.statement: Voyager 2 visited all four outer planets: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.
facts.wowFactor: The Grand Tour alignment that made Voyager 2's route possible happens only once every 175 years. NASA acted fast — and it paid off.
facts.visualDescription: A trajectory line curving through pixel art depictions of four giant planets in sequence against a star-filled void.

facts.wowFactor: Meteorites are time capsules from the solar system's birth. By measuring the decay of radioactive isotopes in them, scientists pinpointed the solar system's age to 4.568 billion years.

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many minutes does light take to travel from the Sun to Earth?
facts.explanation: Light travels at 299,792 km/s, and the average distance from the Sun to Earth (1 AU) is about 150 million km. At that speed, sunlight takes approximately 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach us. This means we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.
facts.statement: Light takes approximately 8 minutes to travel from the Sun to Earth.
facts.wowFactor: When you look at the Sun, you're seeing it as it was 8 minutes ago. If the Sun somehow vanished instantly, we wouldn't know for 8 minutes — and Earth would continue orbiting a ghost star.

facts.quizQuestion: What size category does our Sun belong to among stars?

facts.statement: The Sun is a medium-sized G-type star — not especially large by stellar standards.
facts.wowFactor: The Sun is so massive it could hold 1.3 million Earths — yet it's considered a small to medium star. Stars like UY Scuti are over 1,700 times wider. The universe has unimaginable scales.

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the largest moon in the solar system, bigger even than Mercury?
facts.explanation: Ganymede, a moon of Jupiter, has a radius of 2,631 km — larger than Mercury's 2,440 km radius. It is the largest natural satellite in the solar system, though Mercury has considerably more mass because it is much denser.
facts.statement: Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system, with a radius of 2,631 km — larger than the planet Mercury.
facts.wowFactor: Ganymede is so large that if it orbited the Sun instead of Jupiter, astronomers would classify it as a planet — it is bigger in diameter than Mercury but doesn't quite match its mass.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art size comparison showing Ganymede as a large grey-brown moon next to Mercury as a slightly smaller rocky sphere, with Jupiter in the far background

variants.quizQuestion: Which Jovian moon is larger in diameter than the planet Mercury?
If it orbited the Sun, which moon would be classified as a planet due to its size?

At 2,631 km radius, which moon beats Mercury in diameter?

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the only moon in the solar system known to have its own magnetic field?
facts.explanation: Ganymede generates its own magnetosphere — the only moon in the solar system known to do so. Its magnetic field creates auroras near its poles and forms a small magnetosphere embedded within Jupiter's much larger one.
facts.statement: Ganymede is the only moon in the solar system with its own intrinsic magnetic field, creating auroras at its poles.
facts.wowFactor: Ganymede's magnetic field is so strong that the Hubble Space Telescope can detect its auroras from Earth — a faint rocking glow around a moon 628 million km away.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Ganymede with shimmering blue-purple auroral rings glowing at its poles, set against Jupiter's swirling cloud bands in the background

variants.quizQuestion: Which Jovian moon creates its own auroras because it has an intrinsic magnetic field?

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the only moon in the solar system with a thick atmosphere?
facts.explanation: Titan, Saturn's largest moon, has a thick nitrogen-rich atmosphere with a surface pressure 1.5 times greater than Earth's. It is the only moon in the solar system with a substantial atmosphere.
facts.statement: Titan is the only moon in the solar system with a thick atmosphere — a dense nitrogen haze that hides its surface from view.
facts.wowFactor: Titan's atmosphere is so thick that early Voyager scientists couldn't see its surface at all — and at 1.5 times Earth's atmospheric pressure, you could breathe on Titan if you just had a heat suit and an oxygen mask.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Titan as a hazy orange ball surrounded by thick brownish-orange atmosphere layers, Saturn and its rings visible in the distant background

Which moon is hidden by a thick orange haze that prevented early spacecraft from seeing its surface?

facts.quizQuestion: Which moon has lakes and rivers of liquid methane on its surface?
facts.explanation: Titan is cold enough (about -179°C) for methane to exist as a liquid. It has a complete methane cycle — lakes, rivers, rain, and evaporation — mirroring Earth's water cycle but with methane instead of water.
facts.statement: Titan has lakes and rivers of liquid methane on its surface, cycling through evaporation and rain much like water does on Earth.
facts.wowFactor: Titan is the only world besides Earth with stable liquid on its surface — but instead of water, it rains methane, floods into methane lakes, and evaporates back into its thick orange sky.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Titan's surface showing dark liquid methane lakes reflecting an orange sky, methane rain falling in the background, rocky icy shoreline in the foreground

variants.quizQuestion: Which Saturn moon has a cycle of methane rain, rivers, and evaporation like Earth's water cycle?

facts.quizQuestion: Which moon received the Huygens probe in 2005, the most distant landing ever at that time?
facts.explanation: The Huygens probe, carried to Saturn's system by the Cassini-Huygens spacecraft, descended through Titan's atmosphere and landed on its surface in January 2005. At over 1.2 billion km from Earth, it was the most distant landing ever achieved by a human-made spacecraft.
facts.statement: In 2005, the Huygens probe landed on Titan — Saturn's largest moon — making it the most distant planetary landing in history at the time.
facts.wowFactor: Huygens transmitted data for 72 minutes after landing, sending back the first and only ground-level images from a world in the outer solar system — a hazy orange plain littered with ice pebbles shaped by methane rivers.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Huygens probe descending on a parachute through orange haze, Titan's icy pebble-strewn surface below, Saturn faintly visible through the thick atmosphere above

facts.quizQuestion: Which moon shoots water geysers from its south pole into space?
facts.explanation: Cassini discovered that Enceladus has geysers at its south pole that spray water vapor and ice particles hundreds of kilometers into space. This material feeds Saturn's E ring and confirmed that Enceladus has a liquid water ocean beneath its icy surface.
facts.statement: Enceladus shoots powerful water geysers from its south pole into space, evidence of a subsurface liquid ocean beneath its ice.
facts.wowFactor: Cassini actually flew through Enceladus's geysers and tasted them — detecting water, salts, silica, and complex organic molecules, all the ingredients for life, in material shooting directly out of this tiny moon.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of small pale white Enceladus in space, dramatic blue-white water plumes erupting from its south pole and fanning out into space, Saturn's rings visible in background

facts.quizQuestion: Which tiny moon is one of the most reflective objects in the solar system due to its ice surface?
facts.explanation: Enceladus reflects about 99% of sunlight that hits it, making it one of the most reflective bodies in the solar system. Its surface is coated in fresh white water ice, continuously refreshed by its geyser activity.
facts.statement: Despite being only 252 km in radius — tiny compared to most moons — Enceladus is one of the most reflective objects in the solar system, reflecting nearly all sunlight off its fresh ice surface.
facts.wowFactor: Enceladus is so reflective that it actually gets colder than it should by bouncing away most of the Sun's warmth — making its surface temperature a frigid -201°C despite its internal ocean staying liquid.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of tiny brilliant white Enceladus gleaming brightly against the dark of space, sunlight reflecting off its ice surface so intensely it outshines larger nearby moons

facts.quizQuestion: Which large moon orbits its planet in the opposite direction to the planet's rotation?
facts.explanation: Triton, Neptune's largest moon, orbits in a retrograde direction — backwards relative to Neptune's rotation. This is unique among large moons and is strong evidence that Triton was captured from the Kuiper Belt rather than forming alongside Neptune.
facts.statement: Triton orbits Neptune backward in a retrograde direction — the only large moon in the solar system to do so — suggesting it was captured from the Kuiper Belt.
facts.wowFactor: Triton's retrograde orbit means it is slowly being dragged closer to Neptune by tidal forces and will eventually be torn apart by gravity to form a new ring system — in about 3.6 billion years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art top-down view of Neptune with its spin arrow going one direction and Triton's orbital arrow looping backwards in the opposite direction, stark against deep blue Neptune

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet rotates tilted nearly 98 degrees, rolling on its side?
facts.explanation: Uranus has an axial tilt of about 97.77 degrees, meaning it essentially rolls around the Sun on its side. Scientists think a massive collision billions of years ago knocked it over.
facts.statement: Uranus is tilted nearly 98 degrees — it rolls around the Sun on its side.
facts.wowFactor: Uranus is so tilted that its poles get more direct sunlight than its equator for decades at a time.
facts.visualDescription: Cyan ice-giant planet lying on its side in space, glowing rings encircling it horizontally, small moons dotting a starfield.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet is classified as an ice giant alongside Neptune?
facts.explanation: Uranus and Neptune are both called ice giants because they contain a large fraction of water, ammonia, and methane ices — unlike Jupiter and Saturn, which are mostly hydrogen and helium gas.
facts.statement: Uranus is an ice giant, a distinct class from the gas giants Jupiter and Saturn.
facts.wowFactor: Despite the name 'ice giant,' the ices inside Uranus are actually hot compressed fluids, not frozen solid.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side pixel art of Uranus and Neptune glowing blue-green and cobalt blue, labeled 'Ice Giants' above a starfield.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet was the first ever discovered using a telescope, in 1781?
facts.explanation: William Herschel discovered Uranus in 1781 using a telescope — the first planet found by scientific observation rather than naked-eye astronomy known since antiquity. Neptune was later found in 1846.
facts.statement: Uranus was the first planet discovered with a telescope, by William Herschel in 1781.
facts.wowFactor: Before Herschel's discovery, humans had known of only five planets for thousands of years — all visible to the naked eye.
facts.visualDescription: 18th-century astronomer peering through a brass telescope at a pale cyan disk among stars, quill and notebook beside him.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet's blue-green color is caused by methane in its atmosphere?
facts.explanation: Methane gas in Uranus's upper atmosphere absorbs red wavelengths of sunlight and reflects blue-green light back to our eyes, giving Uranus its distinctive aquamarine color.
facts.statement: Uranus looks blue-green because methane in its atmosphere absorbs red light and reflects blue-green.
facts.wowFactor: Neptune has even more methane than Uranus, yet appears a deeper blue — scientists think an unknown atmospheric component makes Uranus look greener.
facts.visualDescription: Close-up pixel art of Uranus showing smooth aquamarine surface with faint ring arcs, methane molecule icons floating in atmosphere layer.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet's existence was predicted by Le Verrier's calculations before it was observed?
facts.explanation: In 1846, French mathematician Urbain Le Verrier predicted Neptune's position by analyzing irregularities in Uranus's orbit. Johann Galle then observed Neptune exactly where Le Verrier said it would be — a triumph of mathematical astronomy.
facts.statement: Neptune was predicted mathematically by Urbain Le Verrier before it was ever observed through a telescope.
facts.wowFactor: Neptune was located within one degree of Le Verrier's predicted position — gravity equations pinpointed a planet billions of kilometers away from a desk in Paris.
facts.visualDescription: Mathematician at a candlelit desk covered in equations and orbital diagrams, pointing at a deep blue planet through a window to a night sky.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has the strongest winds in the solar system, reaching 2,100 km/h?
facts.explanation: Neptune's winds can exceed 2,100 kilometers per hour — faster than the speed of sound on Earth. Despite being far from the Sun and receiving little heat, Neptune's internal heat drives these ferocious storms.
facts.statement: Neptune has the strongest winds in the solar system, reaching about 2,100 km/h.
facts.wowFactor: Neptune receives 900 times less sunlight than Earth, yet its winds are faster than any storm on any other planet — powered almost entirely by internal heat.
facts.visualDescription: Dark blue planet with white streaking storm bands and a Great Dark Spot vortex, wind-speed readout glowing '2100 km/h' in corner.

facts.quizQuestion: Which planet has a major moon, Triton, that orbits in the opposite direction to the planet's rotation?
facts.explanation: Triton orbits Neptune backwards (retrograde) — the only large moon in the solar system to do so. This strongly suggests Triton is a captured Kuiper Belt Object, not a moon that formed alongside Neptune.
facts.statement: Neptune's moon Triton orbits backward — making it a likely captured Kuiper Belt Object.
facts.wowFactor: Triton's backward orbit is slowly decaying — in about 3.6 billion years it will spiral inward and be torn apart by Neptune's gravity, creating a spectacular ring system.

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the farthest planet from the Sun in our solar system?
facts.explanation: Since Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006, Neptune became the farthest official planet from the Sun, orbiting at about 30 AU — roughly 4.5 billion kilometers away.
facts.statement: Neptune is the farthest planet from the Sun, at about 30 AU, since Pluto's reclassification in 2006.
facts.wowFactor: Light from the Sun takes about 4 hours to reach Neptune — sunlight that takes just 8 minutes to reach Earth.
facts.visualDescription: Solar system map showing the Sun on the far left, planet dots stretching right, Neptune glowing blue at the far edge with a distance marker.

facts.quizQuestion: Which body was reclassified from planet to dwarf planet by the IAU in 2006?
facts.explanation: In 2006, the International Astronomical Union formally defined 'planet,' and Pluto failed one criterion — it has not cleared its orbital neighborhood of other debris. It was reclassified as a dwarf planet.
facts.statement: Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006 after the IAU formally defined what a planet must be.
facts.wowFactor: Pluto's demotion was so controversial that the state of New Mexico officially passed a resolution declaring Pluto a planet within their borders.
facts.visualDescription: Small icy world with a heart-shaped bright region on its surface, a tiny Charon nearby, both floating against a dim star field at the solar system's edge.

facts.quizQuestion: Which solar system body is smaller than Earth's Moon, with a radius of only 1,185 km?
facts.explanation: Pluto's radius is 1,185 km, while Earth's Moon has a radius of 1,737 km. Pluto is significantly smaller than the Moon — one of the reasons it was reclassified as a dwarf planet.
facts.statement: Pluto is smaller than Earth's Moon, with a radius of 1,185 km compared to the Moon's 1,737 km.
facts.wowFactor: Seven moons in the solar system, including our own Moon, are larger than Pluto.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side pixel comparison of Earth's grey Moon and the smaller icy Pluto, with a size bracket clearly showing Pluto is shorter.

facts.quizQuestion: Which dwarf planet has a heart-shaped nitrogen ice region called Tombaugh Regio?
facts.explanation: Images from the New Horizons spacecraft in 2015 revealed a bright heart-shaped feature on Pluto's surface. It was named Tombaugh Regio after Clyde Tombaugh, the astronomer who discovered Pluto in 1930.
facts.statement: Pluto has a giant heart-shaped nitrogen ice plain on its surface called Tombaugh Regio.
facts.wowFactor: The 'heart' on Pluto is roughly the size of Texas and Oklahoma combined, made of nitrogen and carbon monoxide ice that reflects sunlight brilliantly.
facts.visualDescription: Small icy dwarf planet in deep space with a vivid white heart-shaped region gleaming on its surface, New Horizons probe silhouette nearby.

facts.quizQuestion: Which body is both the largest object in the asteroid belt and a dwarf planet?
facts.explanation: Ceres is unique in being classified as both the largest object in the asteroid belt (making up about a third of the belt's total mass) and a dwarf planet. It is the only dwarf planet located in the inner solar system.
facts.statement: Ceres is the largest object in the asteroid belt and also the only dwarf planet in the inner solar system.
facts.wowFactor: Ceres contains so much water ice that it may hold more fresh water than all of Earth's surface water combined.
facts.visualDescription: Grey cratered spheroid floating among tumbling rock asteroids, visibly larger than them all, with a faint dwarf-planet label glowing beneath it.
